facts.fact: Millhaven functions as a grain-and-mill village with market ties strong enough that an itinerant grain seller knows the baker, flour prices, and seasonal route habits.
The plague is moving through ordinary food-and-market life strongly enough that villagers can still mistake it for sickness amid continued trade.
Corin's Crossing and nearby farms are still trading despite cough and short flour shipments, which means commerce is continuing inside partial outbreak conditions.
Bread is the daily staple tying village diet to plague severity; people living on oats or separate stores can show much lighter exposure.
The contaminated supplier pattern uses slightly better prices and repeat deliveries to enter a village as ordinary commerce before anyone thinks to question the grain.
Road-factors can buy grain out of estate storehouses and push it onward under their own tally marks, masking origin behind middleman paperwork.
Flour buyers, bakehouses, mills, and village depots sit at different points in the same distribution chain, so upstream versus downstream position matters to the hunt.
Large buyers and institutional consumers are likely to keep duplicate books, making secondary record holders economically important once depots are scrubbed.
The network can pull tainted loads off a road that has gone hot and let clean grain continue through as commercial cover.
By chapter6 Andorhal's bakery quarter is busy, grain wagons are still arriving under oiled cloth, and the city remains a fully active food-distribution center rather than a disrupted market.
By chapter7 the contamination is confirmed to be introduced during repacking or transfer inside Andorhal's distribution chain, after receipt but before the flour reaches downstream bakehouses.
